pattern: The best score reachable at a spot only depends on the best score among the last k spots, so a sliding-window maximum turns an O(n*k) lookback into a single O(n) pass.
hints: Think of best[i] as the best total you can have standing on spot i; it is nums[i] plus the best of whatever best[i-k..i-1] were, since any of those spots could have jumped straight to i.

Repeatedly scanning the last k best-values for a maximum is what makes the naive approach slow; keep a shrinking/growing window of candidate indices instead so the current maximum is always available in O(1).

Maintain a deque of indices with strictly decreasing best-values: pop expired indices off the front (older than k), read the front for the current maximum, then pop any smaller-or-equal values off the back before pushing the current index.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, mixed signs with k=2
statement example, forced to accept a zero net path
k reaches the far end so the last index is reachable directly
k=1 forces every element to be visited even though all are negative
a single big jump over a deep negative pit beats crawling through it
two equal-height peaks separated by a pit narrower than k

stress.description: large random array with a moderate jump window
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(-10000, 10000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, min(n, 5000))
    return [nums, k]

stress.description: large array with k=1 (worst case for the naive lookback)
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(-10000, 10000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums, 1]
